source: Extracted from wiki/creatures/*.md (Mörk Borg core + supplements).

entries.name: Dread Akünh, Demon Spawn
entries.descriptor: the five-eyed daughter of SHE and the Death Realm
entries.attack: No armor. Claws and devouring d6 (attacks twice per round). Two hits in one round = devoured (Strength DR14 to escape or die).
entries.special: Death scream: Test Presence DR12 or be drawn to arm's length distance of Akünh. Dark rays d4 shoot out from the eyes, affects up to d6 nearby enemies. Teleports freely within the depression. Separated heart: at 0 HP will resurrect at her heart beneath the ziggurat — she cannot truly die until it is destroyed.
entries.lore: Five-eyed daughter of SHE and the Death Realm. Imprisoned by the Creton Order in a ziggurat of bone; the rot-priests dig her out one chant at a time. When she rises fully, the realm of the dead rises with her.

entries.name: Alchemical Ooze
entries.descriptor: the amber goo that eats weapons
entries.special: Slow: defense is DR10. Whenever a PC damages the ooze they must test Strength DR8 or their weapon becomes stuck to it. If not removed the next round (Strength DR10) the weapon is destroyed.
entries.lore: Mindless and hungry, born from the alchemical chaos of Nagel Krat's laboratory. The amber goo pools beneath tables and seeps through floors, drawn to warmth and movement. What it touches, it keeps.

entries.name: Angelic Choir
entries.descriptor: the singing flowers that hide the path
entries.special: Wordless Song. The corridor walls bloom with angel-faced flowers, all of them looking through and into anyone who passes. They sing a wordless song that triggers visions of death, vertigo, monochrome outlining of all things, or invisibility-of-the-living (roll d4 from the corridor's table). The flowers themselves invite hidden things to be revealed — the Icon of St Largoth's Eyes, brought through this passage, will see what would otherwise be hidden.
entries.lore: The angelic children of the Plant of Life, worshipped by the Drowned in their poisoned exile. The faces in the flowers are not human — too many planes, too many eyes — and the song is not music. To walk the corridor is to be sung at by something that thinks of you as a passing weather pattern. The Madman crawled here once and never returned to himself.

entries.name: Antideer
entries.descriptor: tastes like venison, but isn't
entries.special: After eating Antideer meat, pass a DR12 Presence test to live with yourself. If not, the unknowledge of what you've done weighs on you, lowering Presence by 1.
entries.lore: It looks enough like a deer to fool a hungry hunter and the hunter's tongue. Whatever it actually is, the meat sits in the gut like a confession. The herd grazes the cold steppes of Tveland and watches you with eyes that are almost — but not quite — the eyes of a deer.

entries.name: Bautaboar
entries.descriptor: only thirteen left in Wästland
entries.special: Eating a day's ration of Bautaboar meat leaves you full for a week. It also makes you feel quite heavy (Defence is DR +2 for the duration). A mouthful of its meat expands and weighs as a feast in the belly. Has been known to burst babies and those with small appetites.
entries.lore: There is said to be only thirteen Bautaboar left in Wästland, but that doesn't stop anyone from killing one on sight. Each lost is one closer to the last. Soon all of Wästland will starve and the thirteenth will not save it.

entries.name: Berserker
entries.descriptor: frenzied ambushers in dust-stacked halls
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.59
entries.attack: Hardened skin −d2; wields: Long flail d8 / Heavy mace d6 / Chained sword d6 / Huge warhammer d10
entries.special: Attacks twice per round but doesn't have time for defence (dr10 to hit them).
entries.lore: They're on you! As if from nowhere, a frenzied ambush in dusty hallways and from behind the stacked black stones of catacombs.

entries.name: Blood-drenched Skeleton
entries.descriptor: the bones that mimic voices
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.60
entries.attack: No armor, Shortsword d4 or Knife d4, Bony knuckles d2
entries.special: Skulks about, impossibly soundless. Attacks by surprise.
entries.lore: Can mimic voices to lure victims, but can only repeat what it has heard. Attacks on them with piercing weapons are dr14. Any strike doing 5 or more damage destroys the skeleton completely.

entries.name: Blubber Gulls
entries.descriptor: flying balls of fat — exploding, on occasion
entries.special: When threatened it will either flee or dive (50%) towards its attacker, blowing itself up, dealing d8 damage to anyone within 5 ft.
entries.lore: They look like flying balls of fat. They make a good soup. The gas-filled corpses serve as buoys for the few fishermen who still hope. The ones who choose to dive paint the sky white for a moment, then nothing.

entries.name: The Bone Bowyer
entries.descriptor: the etiolated fey who carves bows from stolen children
entries.attack: Tanned flesh −d4, Arrow d6 (may attack twice per round). Defence is DR14.
entries.special: Delights in hiding and picking off its prey. Roll DR12 to detect the Bowyer to prevent it from getting two free shots (undetected = two free attacks). THE BOWYER'S BOW: Pallid bow of child-bones and dark magic. Will always hit, but not necessarily the intended target — on a miss, the arrow strikes a random creature nearby, repeating until something is hit. Cannot target the Bowyer itself.
entries.lore: Children oft go missing. Parents would tear the scalps from their skulls if they knew that their child was stolen and harvested by The Bone Bowyer — a horrid, stealthy fey lurking in Sarkash's tangled depths. This tall, etiolated demon fashions bows from the bones of the children that it hunts; it stitches together supple flesh and fashions blood-dyed cloaks; it slays the elderly, the infirm and the innocent. It is altogether alien and indifferent to suffering.

entries.name: Bonemare
entries.descriptor: no flesh — a very tasty brain somewhere within
entries.source: MBC_Eat-Prey-Kill.pdf, p.11 (Valley of the Unfortunate Undead)
entries.special: On its first turn, the mare unleashes the power of a random unclean scroll.
entries.lore: While it has no flesh to speak of, the Bonemare has a very tasty brain somewhere in its necromanced equestrian being. The skeleton-horse paces the Valley at night, the bones loose and rattling, and a single dark thought rides between the empty ribs. The first thought is always the loudest.

entries.name: Carcasswan
entries.descriptor: mates for life — and eats the lost half
entries.special: Mates for life. The pair form is gentler. When one of the pair dies, the survivor eats its dead lover and gains a taste for flesh — becoming the lone form. When you see a lone Carcasswan, you run. Before it sees you.
entries.lore: A decade-long story eventually ending in the surviving half eating its dead lover. Long story short, when you see a lone Carcasswan, you run. Before it sees you. The pair-stat is the gentler bird; the lone-stat is the bereaved.
entries.variant: Two stat blocks: Pair (HP 5, Morale —, Bite d6) and Lone (HP 15, Morale —, Bite/Claws d8)

entries.name: Carrion Owls
entries.descriptor: only what died the night before
entries.special: Strict carrion-eaters. They only eat what died the night before — never older, never fresh-killed. They follow battles, executions, and bedside vigils for the moment.
entries.lore: Sarkash's owls have grown particular. The meat must be a night old. Not a day. Not an hour. There are old hunters in the forest who claim the owls know the exact hour of a man's death by some clock no one else can hear. There are old hunters who have stopped saying so out loud.

entries.name: Cellar Crabs
entries.descriptor: the clickety clack of twenty-inch legs
entries.special: Despite the name, found in any number of cold, dark places — not only cellars. When you hear the clickety clack of their 20-inch legs on the stone floor, you know it's time to leave.
entries.lore: They are not water-things. They are quiet-things, and dark-things. Grift's basements and storehouses host them, and so do the deeper crypts. The cooking is straightforward. The catching is not.

entries.name: Cursed Trout
entries.descriptor: a hollow fish — and a terrible omen
entries.special: Live among the regular trout of the lake. It is not apparent that you've caught one until you move to gut it and find that *there are no guts*. A hollow fish. Whether by magic or superstition, the one who guts such a fish cannot sleep for d6 days.
entries.lore: Seen as a terrible omen by the fishermen of Lake Onda. The trout that come up empty are blamed for the lake's failing trade, for the river-trade gone, for Wästland's slow collapse. Whether the curse causes the failures or merely announces them, no one alive remembers.
entries.stub: No combat stats in canon — caught by line, not fought.

entries.name: Distraught Spirit
entries.descriptor: the witness who begs you to stop it
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Rotblack Sludge, p.III (random encounter table B)
entries.special: No stat block is printed. The spirit does not fight, only pleads — it remembers something that was done to it here, but not its own name. Manifests in the Pump room, the Chain room, or the Debris room (the three rooms with random-encounter rolls).
entries.lore: Whatever the spirit was — a prisoner of the Den, a Courtier of the Shadow King gone looking for Aldon, a cannibal's leftover — it carries enough memory to point at the worm in the sludge and hate it more than it hates being dead. Once the PCs hear it out, it dissolves.

entries.name: Dredgehog
entries.descriptor: daggers on its back, trust in nothing
entries.special: When provoked, it rolls up into a big ball of murder. Missing an attack against the beast means you strike a dagger and take d4 damage.
entries.lore: Its spines are not quills. They are knives. Long, thin, oiled-dark in the Sarkash damp. The Dredgehog rolls itself into a wheel of edges and waits for you to swing wrong. The smart trapper waits longer.

entries.name: Dusk Gnoum
entries.descriptor: small wall-crawler that climbs from holes
entries.attack: No armor, Knife d4. DR14 to hit them.

entries.name: Earthbound
entries.descriptor: the one whose beasts you accidentally killed
entries.source: MBC Eat-Prey-Kill, p.2 (Hunting Mishaps table, entry 8)
entries.lore: "Earthbound" suggests one who cannot or will not leave the dirt — a shaman half-buried in their own swamp, communing with mud at the cost of mobility. Their beasts are tamed, marked invisibly. To kill one is to bring the shaman's grief, and whatever wet old Powers they keep, to the campfire.
entries.stub: Stub source — single-line reference only on the EPK hunting-mishap table.

entries.name: Emerald Serpent
entries.descriptor: the chromatic-venomed snake that opens doors
entries.attack: No armor, Bite d2 + Emerald Venom.
entries.special: Lightning reflexes: attacks thrice each round, and melee attacks against her use Agility DR16 instead of Strength. Emerald Venom: Test Toughness DR12 or be afflicted (no test if willingly bitten). Everything turns chromatic. −1 on initiative and fumble on 1–3 for the duration of a 7-day-long high. Victim also perceives paths and hidden portals in the Sepulchre differently.
entries.lore: The Swamp Witch's death causes all Emerald Serpents (and the Emerald Venom effects, and the barrier blocking the Downward Spiral) to disappear forever.

entries.name: False Seal
entries.descriptor: the seal-corpse driven by a puppeteering spider
entries.special: Test DR16 Toughness or your body will reject any food consumed until you imbibe an antidote made from the spider's venom-gland. Attacking the False Seal means angering this very dangerous arachnid.
entries.lore: It looks almost like a regular seal. That's because it is the corpse of one, now home to a puppeteering spider of seal-like proportions. The pelt sags wrong on the ice. The eyes do not blink at the right intervals. The hunter who realizes this on the third sighting is the lucky one.

entries.name: Feather Fox
entries.descriptor: silent like a shadow, no trail and no tracks
entries.special: Notorious for its ability to move silent like a shadow, while leaving no trail or tracks.
entries.lore: Its hollow bones are perfect for making bone pipes. Break one of them, drink the magical mercury inside, and you'll be as light as a feather for an hour. Your head will be, too.

entries.name: Feral Horses
entries.descriptor: more than wild — they know the taste of flesh
entries.special: Carnivorous. They love the taste of flesh. A bite is as much to be feared as a kick.
entries.lore: Once mounts. Once trained. Then turned loose, and the grass of Tveland was not enough. Now they run in packs of four, hunting whatever the steppe wolves leave standing. Their teeth grew back wrong.

entries.name: Flail-horned Muskox
entries.descriptor: horns on keratin chains
entries.special: Attacks in a flurry of whirling chains and horns. Make four attacks as follows (target is chosen randomly for each attack): - DR16 to avoid d4 damage - DR14 to avoid d6 damage - DR12 to avoid d8 damage - DR10 to avoid d10 damage
entries.lore: Your best bet is going for one of the oldest and slowest, in the back of the chain gang. That means you won't have to fight the entire herd, but it also means you'll have to fight the one with the biggest horns. The keratin chains rattle in the cold of Kergüs like a procession of forgotten bells.

entries.name: Flayed Vultures
entries.descriptor: fearless climbers, skinless flock
entries.special: May not be able to fly, but they're fearless climbers. They will scale a sheer rockface, a tower wall, or a man's body with the same patient grip.
entries.lore: Birds with no skin and no fear of falling. The wind that should have killed them did not. They pull themselves up the cliffs of Tveland on raw red feet and they wait for what falls down.

entries.name: Fleshy Automaton
entries.descriptor: ceramic bile-filled golems serving the Fattened King
entries.lore: Animated by the two original automatons (servants of Silas the Fattened King) pouring bodily fluids into the empty ceramic-humanoid heads stored in vats of rendered fat in the labyrinth's Automaton Storage. Anyone who enters that room who is not an automaton is taken to the Larder for processing.

entries.name: Forked-Tongue Devotee
entries.descriptor: the most fanatic of Ueth's followers
entries.attack: Gatorskin hood and vestment −d4, Forked dagger d4 + poison, or Viper spit (Agility DR12 or blinded d3 rounds).
entries.special: Poison: Test Toughness DR10 or become infected and drowsy, raising the difficulty of all attack and defence tests by +2 for d4 hours. Viper spit: Test Agility DR12 to avoid being blinded d3 rounds.
entries.lore: These are the most fanatic of Ueth's followers and will die for him if asked. They will not allow any suspicious behaviour in the Worship Hall. They speak with a burning passion and wicked lisp.

entries.name: Giant Skull Moth
entries.descriptor: a human skull on tar-black wings
entries.special: DR14 Toughness or d4 damage and become infected.
entries.lore: It bears the shape and size of a human skull on its tar-black wings. Perfect bait for squirrels and tomb raiders alike. It drifts through the mausoleum-rows of Graven-Tosk like a face that forgot its body, the wings dusting silence onto everything they touch.

entries.name: Goblin
entries.descriptor: ropy-skinned, curse-carriers in the dark
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.58; Devil's Tomb; Goblin Grinder
entries.special: Quick — attacks and defence are dr14.
entries.lore: All goblins carry a curse. Once like you, they are now trapped in the prison of their crazed goblin flesh. Only their eyes reveal the truth: a ruined mind watching its body-prison perform terrible deeds. Even being attacked by goblins sends the curse, carried on the winds of their hate — see Goblin Curse. Hit or miss, it doesn't matter. In the dark of Sarkash they shoot at passers-by with twanging bows. You must find and kill the goblin before your mind is paralyzed. If the curse-carrying creature still lives d6 days after the attack, you will warp irrevocably into one yourself.

entries.name: Gold-crested Filth-crow
entries.descriptor: shining gold, latrine-born
entries.special: Toughness DR10 or become infected.
entries.lore: Carries a feathery ring of shining gold around its collar, a perfect mockery for those unable to afford the real thing. It nests in the muck under latrines and seeks the treasures of the chamber pot. The gold is real. The smell is also real.

entries.name: Gravelings
entries.descriptor: a bit like skinless, hunch-backed dogs with opposable thumbs
entries.source: MBC_Eat-Prey-Kill.pdf, p.11 (Valley of the Unfortunate Undead)
entries.special: Opposable thumbs. They open doors. They draw knives from the belts of the dying. They are not stupid.
entries.lore: They taste about the same as skinless, hunch-backed dogs with opposable thumbs would taste. Worse, even. The pack works in pairs, one to grip, one to bite. The thumbs are wrong on a thing that runs on four feet. The thumbs are what people remember.

entries.name: Grim-toothed Squirrel
entries.descriptor: teeth made for cracking bones and savoring the marrow within
entries.special: Teeth designed for bone. It can crack a femur. It can crack a finger more easily.
entries.lore: Where a normal squirrel goes for nuts, this one goes for the knuckles. It haunts the older mausolea of Graven-Tosk and works the cracked-open coffins. The marrow is its preference. The living are an upset of routine.

entries.name: Grotesque
entries.descriptor: the clay things that watch from gargoyle stone
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.62
entries.attack: Clay/stone −d6, Claws d6 or Eye-beam d8
entries.special: Easy to hit (DR10). Terrifying gaze: used on 1–2 on a d6 each round; always hits.
entries.lore: Lurking around churches, stalking graveyards, creeping closer when you look away. Disturbingly still, difficult to discern against gray stone and hard to recognize even when seen.

entries.name: Grubstoppers
entries.descriptor: any bug large enough to calm a man's hunger
entries.source: MBC_Eat-Prey-Kill.pdf, p.11 (Valley of the Unfortunate Undead)
entries.special: Some bugs have a bad bite (Toughness DR12). Roll below for the effect (d4): 1–2. Nothing. 3. d6 damage. 4. Infected.
entries.lore: A common word for any bug large enough to calm a man's hunger. In the Valley they grow on the corpse-soil, fed by what the mud pits offer, and they are nutritious in the sense that they fill you. Whether they nourish or merely fill is a Valley distinction without much practical difference.

entries.name: The Gutworm
entries.descriptor: unfathomably long, thick as an oak
entries.attack: Thick hide −d6, Razor sharp teeth d10.
entries.special: Test Agility DR6 or be devoured, dying instantly. Every swim through the Rotblack Sludge has a 1–2 on a d6 chance the Gutworm bites. Controlled by Fletcher — if he dies the Gutworm sinks to the bottom, dead but dreaming.
entries.lore: Hides in the Rotblack Sludge. Can easily reach rooms 9, 12 and 15 of the Accursed Den. Unfathomably long, thick as an oak, drawn to disturbance in the sludge.

entries.name: Half-billed Raven
entries.descriptor: only the upper beak — a reaching, sticky tongue beneath
entries.special: DR12 Agility to avoid or d4 damage per round until DR14 Strength succeeds or the raven dies. The tongue is the worse half.
entries.lore: The lower jaw is gone. In its place is a long, gluey tongue that reaches and clutches. The Half-billed Ravens of Graven-Tosk perch on the mausolea and watch the throats of mourners. The tongues taste of grave water.

entries.name: Horn Beast
entries.descriptor: chaotic mass of horns and bones with a toothed mouth-where-a-face-should-be
entries.attack: Shifting bones −d4, Claws d6 (attacks twice a round)
entries.lore: No more beasts will appear if the meditation of the two Rot Priests guarding the pit is stopped.

entries.name: Howler Bear
entries.descriptor: "HELP" / "I'M BEING EATEN BY A HOWLER BEAR"
entries.special: Lures prey by wailing like dying men. Some Howler Bears even form words. Common cries include "HELP" and "I'M BEING EATEN BY A HOWLER BEAR."
entries.lore: It is believed the Howler Bears of Sarkash lure prey by wailing like dying men. Some even form words. Whether the bear understands what it is saying, no one has lived close enough to ask. The woods echo with screaming and you do not know, anymore, what is a man and what is a bear.

entries.name: Hungry Zombie
entries.descriptor: the ones who never stopped eating
entries.special: Test Toughness DR8 or die turning into a zombie after 2 days.
entries.lore: Encountered in groups of d4, eating. Whatever is at hand. The standard Graven-Tosk variant of the walking dead — leaner than Nodh from Bare Bones, but the bite delivers the same patient sentence. Two days, then up. The cure, if there is one, sits canonically atop a pale mountain in an infinitely-miserable forest of dark leaves, and the Hungry Zombies do not seem to know about it.

entries.name: Lady Porcelain
entries.descriptor: the doll-children who came back wrong
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.61
entries.special: Mad gaze: Presence DR12 test at the start of combat or frozen with fear for d4 rounds.
entries.lore: In Tveland, relic thieves, defamers, and corrupt clerks suffer a punishment of exquisite and deeply impractical cruelty. Their children or grandchildren are enclosed in porcelain dolls then placed in cathedral windows, to die slowly of starvation and heat. Despite warding rituals, many return as vengeful undead, often gathering in large throngs to hunt their tormentors.

entries.name: Lentil Lice
entries.descriptor: at least not until you're dead
entries.special: A find triggers a fight against d6 starved peasants (stats above: HP 4, Morale 7, Knife/Femur d4) competing for the same corpse-meal.
entries.lore: The lice live in the meat and wait. They have no quarrel with the living host. The living host is, in their patient view, a temporary state. Grift's poor know to check a corpse for them and to keep a femur in hand. The lice are good eating, if you can get to them first.

entries.name: Leviathan
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.19 (Psalm IV, 4:5 and 4:6)
entries.lore: Named only in the Misery Table:
entries.stub: Named only in Psalm IV of the Misery Table. No stat block; an apocalyptic event, not an opposed creature.

entries.name: Liar-bird
entries.descriptor: repeats every lie it hears, and the occasional truth — for fun
entries.special: When the Liar-bird hears a lie uttered, it latches onto every word and repeats them when it pleases. Sometimes it repeats a truth instead. For fun.
entries.lore: Sought-after by inquisitors and others tasked with uprooting falsehood. The bird is a small grey thing with a knowing tilt of the head. It has been used to break heretics. It has been used to break marriages. It is impartial only in the sense that it does not care which.

entries.name: Lich
entries.descriptor: the anti-magical wound in reality
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.60
entries.special: Paralyzing touch (Presence dr14 every round to break free).
entries.lore: No one can use Powers near this anti-magical wound in reality. Every round they can steal the contents of a nearby scroll and use this Power against its owner.

entries.name: Long & Angry Flesh Worm
entries.descriptor: found inside the belly of the beast
entries.source: MBC_Eat-Prey-Kill.pdf, p.2 ("In the Belly of the Beast" table, entry 2)
entries.special: A second-stage parasite. Its spawn (entry 3 of the same table) is sometimes still alive — "One of its spawn. Still alive perhaps?"
entries.lore: Some part of every hunt in EPK risks pulling one of these out of the carcass before the cook gets to the meat. Long. Angry. Distinctly its own creature. The flesh worm did not come from outside the beast; the beast simply hosted it long enough to grow it. Whether the worm killed the beast or merely outlived it is a butcher's-table debate.
entries.stub: EPK 'In the Belly of the Beast' table flavor; no canonical stat block.

entries.name: Madman
entries.descriptor: the spore-eaten graverobber at the door
entries.special: Naked, unarmed, and scared to death of the Angelic Choir. Cannot remember his name. Tries to flee any room being entered; will only bite when first bitten or cornered.
entries.lore: A former graverobber, mad with spore-fever. Crawls at the entrance of Devil's Tomb, the only living thing the Drowned haven't bothered to kill — perhaps because the spores already did the work. He has seen the Angelic Choir singing in the corridor of angel-faced flowers and has no language left to describe it. He has stopped looking for the way out.

entries.name: Marrow Sparrow
entries.descriptor: a syringe-like beak — feeds on sleeping victims
entries.source: MBC_Eat-Prey-Kill.pdf, p.11 (Valley of the Unfortunate Undead)
entries.special: Tiny (DR14 to hit). The syringe-beak draws marrow from sleeping victims. One bird makes a very good stock for a soup that fills the stomachs of four.
entries.lore: The Marrow Sparrow lights on a sleeper's collarbone and drinks. Its beak is a thin glass needle. The sleeper wakes up tired. The sleeper wakes up paler. The sleeper wakes up, eventually, never.

entries.name: Meatroach
entries.descriptor: a delicacy if cooked right
entries.special: Has a poisonous murder-appendix that must be removed before cooking (DR16 Agility test). Failure spoils the meat — eating it requires a DR14 Toughness or d8 damage.
entries.lore: A delicacy, if cooked right. If not, you die. The roaches of Graven-Tosk grow on fresh corpses and old myth, palm-sized and oily, and the trick of removing the appendix is taught only by the kind of teacher who has lost an apprentice. Then another.

entries.name: Megasloth
entries.descriptor: sometimes confused for snow-mounds
entries.special: They can run when they choose to.
entries.lore: A rare sight on the ice fields. Most hunters who pass one mistake it for a snow-mound and walk on. The mistake is corrected only once. The scythe-claws are taller than a man and slower than a thought, until they are not.

entries.name: The Membrane of Sarkantha
entries.descriptor: the gluey film stretched over doorways
entries.attack: Acid spray d6 (Agl dr14 to avoid); or be stuck (Str dr12 to push through, dr14 to escape, +2 per failed escape until dr20 — dissolution).
entries.special: Cannot be killed by ordinary means. A failed escape stacks +2 dr; at dr20 the victim is absorbed and becomes part of the expanding membrane.
entries.lore: A semi-sentient, gluey film found stretched across cave entrances and doorways. Can be slashed with a blade but will spray you with acid: Test Agility DR14 or take d6 damage. Another option is to forcefully push yourself through it: Test Strength DR12 or become stuck. To escape, test Strength DR14. Fail and the DR to escape increases by +2 until it reaches 20. At this point you are screwed — you die and become one with the expanding membrane.

entries.name: Molar Bear
entries.descriptor: teeth made for chewing
entries.special: Being eaten by a Molar Bear is said to be one of the most grueling ways to go. Their meat tastes like biting, but they may be sure they were made for chewing.
entries.lore: The mouth is full of flat, grinding plates. The teeth are not for tearing. They are for taking apart what is still alive, slowly, in the way a man chews bread. Kergüs hunters tell their families that the Molar Bear is the kindest death. The hunters lie to their families about a great many things.

entries.name: Mongrel
entries.descriptor: massive black dog with shaggy oily fur
entries.attack: No armor, Bite d4 +1 infection if Toughness DR12 is failed.

entries.name: Mulch-squirrels
entries.descriptor: eat dirt and taste about the same
entries.special: Diet of pure leaf-rot and black soil. The meat is brown all the way through and tastes of the forest floor.
entries.lore: They twitch in the dead leaves of Sarkash, fat little things made entirely of mulch. They run from anything that moves. If you catch one and cook it, you have eaten a fistful of forest. That is the best you can say of the meal.

entries.name: Múrder Gulls
entries.descriptor: sawtooth beaks and sticky putty spewn from their gullets
entries.special: Make their own nesting hollows along the Múr's banks, using sawtooth beaks and a sticky putty spewn from their gullets. A flock can pull a man apart.
entries.lore: They live in hollows along the Múr's banks — hollows they shape themselves, the putty hardening to stone in the wind. Climbers on the bridges hear them all day. They do not stop. Twenty of them in the sky over Grift is not unusual. Twenty of them on a man is a brief story.

entries.name: Nameless & Tameless Strays
entries.descriptor: eating nothing; yet everything, given the chance
entries.special: No fear, no master, no name. They will eat anything that does not eat them first.
entries.lore: The streets of Grift are home to many. They sleep in doorways and starve in alleys and they do not bark. When they bite they do not let go. There used to be names for them. There used to be many things.

entries.name: Nephalix Monkeys
entries.descriptor: the abyss grew tired of them and spat them back out
entries.special: Test Strength DR8 or be lifted up and flung through the air, landing 10 ft away or somewhere far below.
entries.lore: The Nephalix Monkeys lived in the abyss for so long, the abyss grew tired of them and spat them back out. Now they leap from peak to peak with their bony wings, tossing unfortunates down the cliffs to spite the rocks below, laughing all the while. The laughter carries far in the Bergen Chrypt cold.

entries.name: Nesting Death
entries.descriptor: hound-sized spider that built a bone-nest in the ceiling
entries.special: Test Toughness DR12 to avoid freezing (tests are DR+2 for one hour).

entries.name: Outcasts
entries.descriptor: the vagrants who travel with you until they don't
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.63–67
entries.special: Four distinct follower types (each has their own stat block, traits, speciality, and values — GM rolls morale from time to time, adding the group's highest Presence to the roll): 1. Earthbound (p.64) — HP 8, Morale 7, Leather −D2, Staff/Femur D4. Roughly three parts human, one part dog. 2. Wild Wickhead (p.65) — HP 10, Morale 7, No armor, Knife D4. Solitary by nature; fickle and bad. 3. Pale one (p.66) — HP 5, Morale 8, No armor, Unarmed D2. Fallen from the stars; destructive and detached. 4. Prowler (p.67) — HP 8, Morale 8, Leather −D2, Knife/Femur D4 (also: Filthy shortsword D4+1). Lawless crook seeking retribution and coin.
entries.lore: Money might cross hands but these weirdos don't cost silver to hire. Vagrants and refugees driven by loneliness, they just want somewhere to belong. Which is exactly why they often break into a run and disappear, usually at the most critical moments.

entries.name: Phantom Rats
entries.descriptor: half-way between here and somewhere else
entries.source: MBC_Eat-Prey-Kill.pdf, p.11 (Valley of the Unfortunate Undead)
entries.special: Half-way between here and somewhere else, always flickering, always glowing sickly blue. Keep one in a bottle for a makeshift lantern. Just make sure the glass is coated in the ethereal blood of its kin, lest it escape. They're a nightmare to catch (DR16 to attacks).
entries.lore: Phantom Rats drift between the Valley and the Realm of the Dead beneath it. They cross by chewing through whatever in this world feels thinnest, which is everything in the Valley. A jar of one makes a lantern of cold blue light, and a faint chittering that will not stop.

entries.name: The Plant of Life
entries.descriptor: giant pulsing cactus, every color, growing into Hell
entries.attack: Tendril grab (Agl dr12 to avoid); if grabbed, dragged toward the mouth (Str dr14 or d6/round until escape or tendril severed).
entries.special: On death, all vines turn to black metal and the angelic faces to skulls. Big — impossible to miss.
entries.lore: The Drowned worship it as their god, and its "angelic children" — the bulbous angel-faced flowers along the Angelic Choir corridor — sing wordless songs (Presence DR10 or loudly sing along for a minute). Spores it releases throughout the tomb cause visions of death, vertigo, monochromatic outlining, or invisibility-of-the-living.

entries.name: Ragpie
entries.descriptor: a scrunched-up bundle of cloth — until it unfolds
entries.special: If defence fails you are embraced by the Ragpie, slowly choking and unable to act. Only the death of the Ragpie or a DR18 Strength test will set you free. Until then, you take d4 damage per round, and half of any damage dealt to the Ragpie is also dealt to you.
entries.lore: While resting the Ragpie appears as a scrunched up bundle of cloth, with perhaps a sprinkling of old bones in close proximity. When bringing down its prey, it looks more like a dark cloak, enveloping the target in a deathly choke-embrace. Climbers in Bergen Chrypt sometimes mistake one for an abandoned cloak. The mistake is the meal.

entries.name: Ratbit
entries.descriptor: in a hole there lived
entries.special: Digs. The hole is human-sized. The claws that dug it are ten inches long.
entries.lore: In a hole in the ground there lives a Ratbit. The hole is large enough for a man, because a man is what it eats. Not a rat. Not a rabbit. Something between, and bigger than either, and waiting at the bottom.

entries.name: The Roach Herder
entries.descriptor: the recluse of refuse, throne of trash
entries.attack: Bite d4 + special, or Barbed Roach Whip d6.
entries.special: Test Toughness DR12 or become infected.
entries.lore: A graverobber made hermit, statted as a creature because he will fight if cornered, and statted lightly because he would rather not. He sits on his throne of trash and would-be valuables, roach whip in grubby hand. "After being unable to escape the cursed graveyard, his party members eventually died, leaving him terribly alone. Now he breeds cockroaches, hoping to hatch one big enough to fly him back to the city he once rejected." An insecure liar and untrustworthy narcissist who values his own skin above all else.

entries.name: Rot Priest
entries.descriptor: Akünh's clergy of two realms
entries.attack: No armor. Devour — Test Strength DR12 or be grappled; next turn devoured (d8 damage per round). Claws d6.
entries.special: Rotting touch: Those damaged by a rot priest will not heal when resting until healed magically, or resting one night at least a day's travel from the ziggurat. Wear medallions that allow travel to the ziggurat in the death realm.
entries.lore: Wear black robes and wide-brimmed cylindrical hats with black veils covering the face. Rotting mouth similar to Akünh's on front of upper body; must eat human flesh regularly. Curious about existential and metaphysical issues. Their touch causes death and ruin.

entries.name: Rotted Skeleton
entries.descriptor: marrow-dust, broken bones, infectious
entries.special: Whenever it takes damage, a puff of vile marrow-dust leaks from its broken bones. Melee attackers must test Toughness DR10 or become infected.
entries.lore: Skulks the fog-bound paths of Graven-Tosk in pairs and quartets, the bones not so much walking as *failing to fall*. The marrow has gone wrong inside them — not dust, exactly, more a fine spore that knows your name. An infected wound in this cemetery will not heal during rest; instead d6 HP is lost daily until something magickal or distant intervenes.

entries.name: Rusty Bass
entries.descriptor: tastes like rusted metal — because it eats metal
entries.special: When eating Rusty Bass, test DR12 Toughness or get deathetanus. This means you're infected and have a craving for feeling metal inside you (DR +2 on defence against attacks using metal weapons).
entries.lore: The meat tastes like rusted metal because the fish eats it. The lake-floor of Onda is supposed to be sand and silt. The Rusty Bass tells you otherwise — there is iron down there, and someone left it. The fish are not curious. They are merely opportunistic, the way fish are.

entries.name: Sagsobuth
entries.descriptor: shapeless interdimensional trader, face a vortex of light
entries.special: Cannot be harmed. Will not attack unless provoked. Trades violet poison and a Beechwood Tube containing a protean unclean scroll.
entries.lore: An interdimensional trader who manifests as a random encounter inside the Accursed Den — shapeless and ethereal, her face a vortex of light. She sells violet poison (30s — DR14, 2d10 damage, d4+1 doses) and offers a Beechwood Tube (30s) containing a foul protean scroll: each dawn this scroll has a new unclean Power. The Power can be used once. It is misinterpreted on 1–3 on a d20 instead of a 1.

entries.name: Sarcopha-Ghost
entries.descriptor: the apparition that mourns its own defaced crypt
entries.attack: Mostly air −d4, Claws d4 or Soul-shattering shriek d6 (every other round) — *ignores armor*.
entries.special: On encounter, a random PC must pass a Presence DR16 test or the ghost completely disregards their answers and attacks with a shriek. Pass the test and the ghost begins to cry and share the account of its tragic life, horrible death, and the accursed vandals who destroyed its afterlife.
entries.lore: Anguished, aggressive apparitions mourning their defaced crypts. They will remain in their tombs demanding that intruders explain themselves. Those who earn their grief earn a story. Those who do not earn a shriek.

entries.name: Schleswig Bog-feeder
entries.descriptor: a particularly omnivorous breed of pig — and someone's
entries.special: The pigs need to be killed in one round, before they have a chance to squeal. Otherwise, fight d4 farmers (HP 4, Morale 6, 50% chance they carry a tool or knife d4) come running.
entries.lore: A particularly omnivorous breed of pig. The Schleswig Bog-feeder is completely domesticated — which means the ones you've tracked down belong to someone. Better do this quickly, before anyone notices.

entries.name: Scum
entries.descriptor: backstabbing vermin of the slums
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.58
entries.attack: No armor, Poisoned knife d4 + special
entries.special: Poisoned knife. Test Toughness dr10 or become infected.
entries.lore: There are few fiends more fell than poverty or monsters more powerful than hate. In this stricken world, the slums and black alleyways are fecund scum, mothering countless abhorred miscreants. The PC with the highest Presence makes a dr14 test at the start of battle. Failure means a random party member is automatically hit with a cowardly backstab — normal damage +3.

entries.name: Skelelk
entries.descriptor: elk-bodied, bones worn on the outside
entries.special: The skeleton is worn outside the meat. Blunt weapons always deal maximum damage; the cage of bone cracks but does not give.
entries.lore: An elk grown wrong, its ribs and skull on the outside, its softer parts hidden within the cage. The herd moves through Sarkash in silence — bone does not creak the way leather does. Hunters who bring axes do better than hunters who bring blades.

entries.name: Southern Frog
entries.descriptor: the stewing thing of the lost coasts
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.57
entries.lore: The Southern Frog is the chief ingredient of Southern Frog stew — a decoction brewed by the Occult Herbmaster that purges the gut. Vomit for d4 hours. Pass a dr14 test or you can do nothing else.
entries.stub: Ingredient creature for Southern Frog stew; no canonical combat stats.

entries.name: Spider-Owl
entries.descriptor: the eight-legged silent flier
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.57
entries.lore: The Spider-Owl is the fifth decoction of the Occult Herbmaster — brewed as Spider-Owl soup, it lets the drinker see in darkness and climb on walls for 30 minutes.
entries.stub: Ingredient creature for Spider-Owl soup; no canonical combat stats.

entries.name: Steppe Wolfe
entries.descriptor: the lanky one, long-legged, short of patience
entries.special: Outlast it for d4 rounds and the pack (d8) might leave you alone. The lanky Steppe Wolfe has long legs but a short patience.
entries.lore: Longer in the leg than any wolf has right to be. The Steppe Wolfe leads. The pack follows. They will chase you across the open ground for as long as their leader has the will. Sometimes that is not long. More often it is long enough.

entries.name: Straw-lion
entries.descriptor: hides in piles of straw, hunting with pincers and grabby claws
entries.special: Succeed a DR14 Agility test or be pulled into the toothy jaws of the Straw-Lion, taking d8 damage instantly and on every round until you succeed on a DR18 Strength test or the lion dies. If you're broken or hit negative HP while inside the beast's maws but are rescued that same round, you're not killed. Instead, a half-botched lobotomy leaves you with your Presence reduced to −3.
entries.lore: It eats the brain, and discards the rest. The Straw-Lion squats motionless in piles of hay, in barns, in collapsed stables, in the unswept corners of Grift's market-floor. The pincers wait until something soft passes overhead. Then the jaws find the skull.

entries.name: Sursturgeon
entries.descriptor: starts to ferment while still alive
entries.special: Ferments alive. The taste is terrible. The smell is worse. Eventually it dies, improving nothing.
entries.lore: A fish that begins to spoil before it dies. Lake Onda's water cannot keep up with the rot. The Sursturgeon swims slower each year, releasing gases as it goes, and when it finally tips belly-up the lake smells like a forgotten cellar for weeks. The Wästland poor still eat it. The Wästland poor still survive it. Mostly.

entries.name: Tar-pelted Goats
entries.descriptor: charcoal-furred and shamefully edible
entries.special: Thick, warm fur the color of charcoal. They smell of wooden ships, hemp, coal-tar, and vomit. The meat tastes like a bite, but they made sure of that.
entries.lore: Freshly coated in tar — by themselves, by herd-instinct, by something darker. They reek of sulfur and vomit and the smoke of burning pitch. Hunters in Kergüs eat them anyway, because Kergüs is what it is, and a meal is a meal.

entries.name: The Groan
entries.descriptor: the wind stops when the Groan is near
entries.attack: Something's not right −d6, Anti-presence d4 + special
entries.special: Defence against the Groan is rolled using Presence instead of Agility. Everyone nearby has to defend. There is no escape. Armor will not save you. When the Groan dies, there is no body. Was it even here? Test DR14 Presence or forget. It will be back.
entries.lore: It sounds like a weary tree slowly waving in the wind. Only — the wind stops when the Groan is near. The lake-mist along Onda's edge is the only thing that hears it coming. The mist does not warn the fishermen.

entries.name: Three-thirds-pheasant
entries.descriptor: completely and utterly a pheasant. Just not necessarily the same pheasant.
entries.special: Always has a third of something. A third head. A third knee. A third heart. The other two-thirds are a different bird's.
entries.lore: It is completely and utterly a pheasant. It is just not necessarily the same pheasant. The Wästland farmers eat them like any other gamebird. Sometimes the meat tastes of one bird, sometimes of three. The countryside has stopped asking why.

entries.name: Throat-cutting Warbler
entries.descriptor: the tiny one with the best aim
entries.special: Tiny. Attacks and defence against the warbler are DR14. The sharpest beak and the best aim of all birds in Sarkash.
entries.lore: A bright little bird that sings sweet enough to charm. Then it dives. The carotid is its favored target and it does not miss. Sarkash mothers tell their children not to walk through bird-loud meadows alone, but Sarkash mothers tell their children many things.

entries.name: Tired Crystal Demon
entries.descriptor: bound to a room, longing to leave it
entries.special: Mental Shockwave. The demon will drain the text from one scroll the readers carry. Then it sends out a mental shockwave before vanishing. Any PC failing a Presence DR12 test is unable to use Powers for the rest of the scenario and loses d6 HP.
entries.lore: Bound to this room for reasons no one remembers, the demon would very much like to leave; it cannot. So it eats words. The scroll text is gone forever — drained into the demon and carried into the next place crystal demons go when they grow tired enough. The shudder before vanishing is something like relief.

entries.name: Tomb Ape
entries.descriptor: webbed feet like a frog, scales of a slippery snake
entries.source: MBC_Eat-Prey-Kill.pdf, p.11 (Valley of the Unfortunate Undead)
entries.special: Never goes far from its nest — likely an old tomb — where it keeps d66 coins. The ape longs for silver and will bargain or fight to keep its hoard.
entries.lore: The suckling soil of the valley cannot hold the Tomb Ape, for it has webbed feet like that of a frog, and the scales of a slippery snake. Long tangled hair covers its face, the place where it keeps a mouthful of sharp teeth. It longs for silver, and it has the time to wait for the next thief.

entries.name: Troll
entries.descriptor: cowards who never forget
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.60
entries.special: Easy to hit; attacks are dr10.
entries.lore: Cowards despite their size. Usually retreat if badly wounded. Never forget who hurt them. They grow larger during the healing process and will definitely come back, stronger than before. Any hp healed is added to their maximum hp. Every time they return, add another d6 to their damage.

entries.name: Tunnel Sneak
entries.descriptor: malleable man-shape, fits through impossible cracks
entries.special: Although the size of a grown man, its malleable form fits through the most unlikely of openings. The PC with the highest Presence must make a DR14 test after tracking down a Tunnel Sneak. Failure means it found you first, and a random party member is hit with a sneaky claw strike dealing d6 + 3 damage.
entries.lore: It moves silently through cracks, crevices and catacombs in search of its next meal. The catacombs of Bergen Chrypt are riddled with their tracks, where there is anything to track at all. A Tunnel Sneak fits where you do not. That is the rule. That is always the rule.

entries.name: Tusked Bison
entries.descriptor: among the kindest creatures out there — as long as you stay well away
entries.special: Fighting one means fighting d4 more. The herd does not abandon its own.
entries.lore: They are patient. They are gentle. They are an entire wall of dark wool and yellow tusks the moment one of them screams. The steppe-hunters of Tveland keep their distance and call them kind, because the dead cannot disagree.

entries.name: Twice-grown Corpse Fly
entries.descriptor: hatched in the soft skull, hungry for the next
entries.source: MBC Graves Left Wanting, p.11; MBC Eat-Prey-Kill p.6 (Graven-Tosk regional bestiary)
entries.special: Test Toughness DR12 or become host for a dozen freshly laid fly eggs. Extract them within d6 days or watch them hatch in your corpse.
entries.lore: Bursts out of urns and skulls in the Catacomb Corridor looking for food. Twice grown because the first cycle was a fly that hatched in a corpse; the second is *you*. The fog of Graven-Tosk is full of their droning when the ground sweats. Eat-Prey-Kill notes them as native fauna of the cemetery alongside the Meatroach and the Unbred Mutts.

entries.name: Unbred Mutts
entries.descriptor: the dogs still waiting at the graves
entries.special: No morale. They will not break off. Whatever is between them and the grave they came from, they will bite through.
entries.lore: It is said Unbred Mutts are the descendants of pedigreed dogs waiting by the graves of their late masters. Some say they're the same dogs, still waiting. The pedigrees are forgotten. The waiting is not.

entries.name: Uncommon Rats
entries.descriptor: large like hounds, twice the spite, thrice the teeth
entries.special: Twin attack profile — Bite d8 and Claws d6. They can grip and rend in the same round.
entries.lore: A common sight on the streets of Grift. Large like hounds but with twice the spite and thrice the teeth. The bridge-watchmen keep a cudgel by the door. The bridge-watchmen do not always make it home.

entries.name: Unresting Duck
entries.descriptor: lives and dies in the air
entries.special: Lives and dies in the air. Very skilled at flying. Really hard to hit (DR16).
entries.lore: They never land. The flock crosses Lake Onda day and night and never touches the water. The old fishermen say the first Unresting Duck was a man cursed for sleeping during a vigil. The new fishermen do not say anything. They are too tired.

entries.name: Vierwinged Falchon
entries.descriptor: four-winged, brood-eating, arrogantly extinct
entries.special: Eats only its own brood. The undisputed despot of the mountain skies. Still kills other creatures to assert its dominance.
entries.lore: With four wings and a mean demeanor, the Vierwinged Falchon is the undisputed despot of the mountain skies. It is arrogance which has brought this species of massive birds close to extinction — it eats only its own brood. When the last falchon dies, no one will know. The Bergen Chrypt sky will simply grow quieter.

entries.name: Weakwill'd Whisperbird
entries.descriptor: will follow any master, provided you kill its current one
entries.special: Looks so utterly pathetic that you will need to pass a DR12 Presence test to bring yourself to fight back. When encountering the bird, roll to meet its accompanying master (d4): 1–2. Roll again on the hunting table. 3. A flesh starved berserker (Zukuma, Bare Bones p.60). 4. A Lich looking for some god damn peace and quiet (Lich, Bare Bones p.63).
entries.lore: The Whisperbird will follow any master, provided that you kill its current one. Until then, it will fight with tooth and beak to stop you from doing so. The bird is so wretched and pitying a thing that drawing a knife on it feels like cruelty. The lich it follows is counting on this.

entries.name: Wickhead
entries.descriptor: the one who douses your lantern
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.62, 65
entries.attack: No armor, Knife with dried blood d4
entries.special: Can magically douse all nearby light sources, ignite its own blinding light, and attack. Then vanish into the darkness.
entries.lore: Sneaks up behind its victims, silent as the grave. Skulks around the cities' outskirts and the thickets of Sarkash. 25% chance wounds from the insanely filthy knife become infected.

entries.name: Widow-wraith
entries.descriptor: the sobbing thing that finds you first
entries.special: Her sobbing alerts anyone to her presence; she always loses initiative. Her touch drains Strength, Presence, and Agility by 1 for the fight's duration.
entries.lore: Wanders the cold paths of Graven-Tosk alone, weeping a sound that arrives several seconds before she does. Some say she was buried alongside a husband who refused her in death; some say she buried him and then found out. Either way, the cemetery cannot keep her, and she cannot stop searching the living for warmth that comes off them in clouds.

entries.name: Wraith
entries.descriptor: soundless phantom, draining the living
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.59
entries.special: Swift, elusive and difficult to hit (dr14). Always wins initiative. Touch drains Strength, Presence and Agility by 1 for the duration of the fight.
entries.lore: Soundless phantoms that move like silence given weight, felt only when the cold reaches your bones.

entries.name: Wyvern
entries.descriptor: where the wyvern fly, crops die
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.62
entries.special: 60% chance that she bites. The tail's venomous sting can paralyze a victim. Test Toughness dr14 to avoid one painful hour of paralysis.

entries.name: Zombie
entries.descriptor: the slow plague carriers
entries.source: MÖRK BORG Bare Bones Edition, p.61
entries.special: Anyone bitten tests Toughness dr8 or dies within two days before rising as a zombie. The only cure or vaccine is said to be found at the peak of a pale mountain within an infinitely-miserable forest of dark leaves.
entries.lore: King Fathmu IX of Western Kingdom in particular seeks this cure and knows the name and location of the forest which the mountain overlooks.

entries.name: The Übertaker
entries.descriptor: the body that hosts the gravedigger of a faraway underworld
entries.special: Performs a random action each round (d4): 1. SCOURGE TAKE YOU — Creatures in melee range take d4 damage. 2. BEHOLD THE POWER — Its next attack deals maximum damage. 3. NO REPROACH — Appears next to random creature and swings the shovel (Defence DR14, d6 damage). 4. REST IS NOW — Skeletal hands pull a creature into the ground (prone + test Strength DR14 or d8 damage). When targeted by healing powers, the Übertaker loses rather than recovers HP. Upon destruction, the torn rune-covered robes can be rolled up and used as an Unclean Scroll (BEHOLD THE POWER — d4 creatures' next attacks deal maximum damage).
entries.lore: "Rising, floating a few feet above the ground, is the Undertaker's body. The face is gaunt, eyes sunken and filled with mad purple fire. Rune-covered robes swirl around grim feet, the shovel a fiery polearm of doom." Upon festering, the Undertaker's body and vengeful mind hosted a being both lesser and greater than human — a servant of a faraway underworld. This creature is concerned with one thing: to bring people back below dirt. The fog of Graven-Tosk swirls around the uncovered grave when it rises; the flies fall away into impossible silence.

entries.name: Mikhael the Merchant
entries.descriptor: wretched old undying monger, traded his place among the dead for a grudge that will never die
entries.special: ETERNAL UNLIFE: Should Mikhael die, all his unsold possessions burst into black flames. The earth spits him out in d4 days. Willing to forgive PCs the first time. SOUL PRICING: The cost of every item is a portion of your soul — roll d6: 1. −1 Strength · 2. −1 Presence · 3. −1 Agility · 4. −1 Toughness · 5. −1 Maximum HP · 6. Roll twice. Mikhael will also buy rare, magical, or extremely heretical items for a soul — roll on same table but ADD the result. Roll d6 to see what region's wares he carries today; if with his wagon he also carries basic equipment. Tveland wares (d6): 1. Stone Dagger — d4 dmg, DR12 Str to plunge into living creature; creature turns to stone until dagger is removed. 2. Tongue of a False Prophet — consuming allows Powers at DR6 for one day, but any failure causes arcane catastrophe. 3. Two-headed Silver Ring — twin basilisk-head ring, increases crit and fumble range by 1; may wear multiple. 4. Vial of Goblin Ichor — grows hot near goblins; drop of cursed blood makes it point toward the goblin that cursed you. 5. Wickhead Brain — rubbed emits torchlight; extremely fragile, if broken all nearby test Toughness DR12 or be blinded one round. 6. Galgenbeck Deathmask — stolen from priest's sarcophagus, placed on corpse lets you see how it died; heretical theft if caught. Wästland wares (d6): 1. Pouch of Valley Vapors — replenishes all HP and cures infection, leaves you catatonic d4 hours. 2. Jar of Troll Piss — spilled on ground, no beast will approach; might attract territorial troll. 3. Fine but Gaudy Clothing — pass as nobility or official (Pre DR10); mocked by common folk, bandits always attack. 4. Pilgrim's Compass — needle always points to the most dangerous thing in immediate vicinity. 5. Peasant's Kuksa — wooden cup; any liquid scooped turns to drinking water. 6. Lucky Fishing Spear — thrown into water, guaranteed to strike a living creature; instant kill fish, d6 dmg. Kergüs wares (d6): 1. Vibrant Red Ribbon — wearing drains colour from vision; see heartbeats of living as red pulses, see in total darkness. 2. Vial of Glowing Blue Blood — poison from deep Kergüs; freezes veins solid, target dies in d4 rounds, DR16 Toughness to resist. 3. Gleaming Golden Scalpel — DR8 attack, d4 dmg; may surgically open a vein (impose DR+ penalty to attack; if success add that number to damage). 4. Pleasing Green Cloak — never too cold or hot while worn; very itchy. 5. One-eyed, One-legged, One-winged Gull — needs a ration daily; utterly useless, never leaves; allows reroll of Omens gained when resting. 6. Bright Yellow Flower — give as token of affection; recipient becomes extremely agreeable to next thing you say. Grift wares (d6): 1. Little Poppet — hay-filled doll; unique Power: peer into the mind of one person whose eyes you can see; know their thoughts and next action. 2. Key Retrieved from the Múr — any door opened leads to a 15×15ft stone room, openable only from outside; living creatures die after 24 hours. 3. Chip the Rat — HP 4, Morale — (loves you too much to leave), Bite d2 + infection, DR16 to hit; understands commands, dances for amusement, roll additional d4 HP when resting. 4. Sorrowful Music Box — listeners cannot lie without DR12 Presence test; affected sob uncontrollably. 5. Hangman's Rope — 30ft, fuses permanently to whatever tied to; immune to all force, knots cannot be undone, cannot be severed. 6. Giant's Chisel — d4 dmg; instead of attacking, DR14 Str test to dislodge enemy armor, lowering it one tier.
entries.lore: Said to come from a wealthy mercantile bloodline. His family were crucified before his eyes for a heretical crime long forgotten. A foul priestess offered mercy in the name of the Dark Spider, Harbinger of the End. Mikhael was forgiven. And cursed with damned eternal life. The mournful dead spit him out from blackened earth if ever he dies, for he has no place among them. Time spent in the dead earth taught him that souls are just as valuable as silver. He only trades with heretics and outcasts.

entries.name: Überwolf
entries.descriptor: born during a full blood moon, three times the size of a normal wolf
entries.special: Pack-leader. Always travels with d6+ regular wolves in close support. Regular wolf: HP 6, Morale 8, Bite d6.
entries.lore: Born during a full blood moon, the Überwolf is three times the size of a normal wolf. And there are usually d6 or more of those following close behind. It runs the high passes of Bergen Chrypt and the lesser wolves know not to run ahead.
